Attention

**If you think you have shared sensitive information:** make sure to reset the exposed credentials and let us know by contacting support@hpc.cam.ac.uk. In your e-mail, please provide a description of what happened, including as much relevant information as possible.

### What Can I Do?[¶](#what-can-i-do "Permalink to this headline")

The following items should help offer some clarity on how to engage with the CSD3/Dawn support team. Please consult and follow this advice.

1. **Communicate clearly with support:** when contacting the Service Desk, describe your original issue rather than the diagnosis or solution suggested by an AI assistant. Be sure to include the **full context** of the problem in your ticket.
2. **Be explicit:** use a descriptive subject line. In the body of your email, provide as much relevant information as possible and avoid pasting errors or log files without context. Users should include any error or log files, submission scripts, error messages, and any relevant commands used that would help the support team to retrace your steps and diagnose the fault.
3. **Cite AI use when relevant:** if AI suggestions informed your troubleshooting, mention this to Service Desk staff so they can identify and correct any misinformation. You won’t be penalised for telling us you’ve used AI to troubleshoot your problem.
4. **Prioritise official documentation:** treat AI advice as a potential aid, not an authority. The official CSD3/Dawn documentation and Service Desk are your primary sources of truth and troubleshooting for all technical

# PI Dawn Access[¶](#pi-dawn-access "Permalink to this headline")

## PI Accessing Portal[¶](#pi-accessing-portal "Permalink to this headline")

**Invitation E-mail:**

You will receive an email from airr-admin@hpc.cam.ac.uk. This email will contain a link to access the portal; please click on that link to proceed.

[![RAC Invitation Email](../_images/waldur_invitation_email.png)](../_images/waldur_invitation_email.png)

## Frequently asked questions (FAQ)[¶](#frequently-asked-questions-faq "Permalink to this headline")

* I am unable to login to multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk and now I get “Connection refused”.

  > + Make sure you are using the same userid, password or public key as you would to login.hpc.cam.ac.uk. As with the login nodes, failing to authenticate three times in succession to multi will cause your address to be blocked for 20 minutes, so if this happens please wait this long before trying again.
* I am using MS Authenticator but the TOTP code doesn’t work.

  > + If you still have the QR code and secret key on your screen then delete the account in your Authenticator and recreate it, making sure that the account is of the **Other** type (not *Personal* or *Work*). Otherwise please contact *support@hpc.cam.ac.uk* to request reset of the MFA secret before trying again, and select account type **Other**.
* When performing ssh to multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk the QR code is replaced either by garbage or is broken up by blank lines, making it unusable.

  > + Setting **export LANG=en\_GB.UTF-8** in Mac terminals before performing the ssh may stop this happening - however if this has already happened you will need to contact *support@hpc.cam.ac.uk* to request reset of the MFA secret before trying again.
* Connection sharing doesn’t work with Filezilla and I keep seeing TOTP requests during file transfers.

  > + Please prefer to use the RDS gateway *rds.uis.cam.ac.uk* for file transfers. This will request TOTP less frequently.
* I don’t have a

This was caused by keepalived deciding to drop its internal routing IP. Packets could come in, but wouldn’t come out, functionally bottlenecking the sftp gateways.
Annoyingly, it left no notification of this happening, or any record of the change, so I can’t tell when this occurred or why.

I’ll have to rebuild these machines soon anyway - they’re EL7.

Anyway - this should be fixed now. My external connections to [rcs.hpc.cam.ac.uk](http://rcs.hpc.cam.ac.uk) work, which is an alias for the same DNS entry as [rcs.uis.cam.ac.uk](http://rcs.uis.cam.ac.uk) .

Attention

**If you think you have shared sensitive information:** make sure to reset the exposed credentials and let us know by contacting support@hpc.cam.ac.uk. In your e-mail, please provide a description of what happened, including as much relevant information as possible.

### What Can I Do?[¶](#what-can-i-do "Permalink to this headline")

The following items should help offer some clarity on how to engage with the CSD3/Dawn support team. Please consult and follow this advice.

1. **Communicate clearly with support:** when contacting the Service Desk, describe your original issue rather than the diagnosis or solution suggested by an AI assistant. Be sure to include the **full context** of the problem in your ticket.
2. **Be explicit:** use a descriptive subject line. In the body of your email, provide as much relevant information as possible and avoid pasting errors or log files without context. Users should include any error or log files, submission scripts, error messages, and any relevant commands used that would help the support team to retrace your steps and diagnose the fault.
3. **Cite AI use when relevant:** if AI suggestions informed your troubleshooting, mention this to Service Desk staff so they can identify and correct any misinformation. You won’t be penalised for telling us you’ve used AI to troubleshoot your problem.
4. **Prioritise official documentation:** treat AI advice as a potential aid, not an authority. The official CSD3/Dawn documentation and Service Desk are your primary sources of truth and troubleshooting for all technical

retrieval_trace.text:
# Sapphire Rapid Nodes with High Bandwidth Memory[¶](#sapphire-rapid-nodes-with-high-bandwidth-memory "Permalink to this headline")

*These new nodes entered general service in October 2024.*

This set of Sapphire Rapid Nodes have [High Bandwidth Memory](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/High_Bandwidth_Memory) built in and are optimised for applications requiring large data bandwidth.

**These nodes are for the exclusive use of the UKAEA (UK Atomic Energy Agency).**

## Slurm partitions[¶](#slurm-partitions "Permalink to this headline")

* These nodes are named according to the scheme *cpu-r-hbm-[1-84]*.
* These nodes are split equally between two Slurm partitions, **ukaea-spr-hbm** (nodes 1-42) and **ukaea-spr-hbm-flat** (nodes 43-84), corresponding to two memory configuration modes, *HBM-only* for the former and *Flat(1LM)* for the latter. See the [Intel configuration manual](https://cdrdv2-public.intel.com/787743/354227-intel-xeon-cpu-max-series-configuration-and-tuning-guide-rev3.pdf) for more details about these memory setups.

> Existing UKAEA-CPU projects will be able to submit jobs to both of these.

* These nodes have **112 cpus** (1 cpu = 1 core), and **9.1 GiB RAM per cpu** for a grand total of 1 TiB RAM per node. The nodes on the **-flat** partition have even more memory: **10.3 GiB RAM per CPU** for a grand total of 1.15 TiB RAM.
* These nodes are interconnected by Mellanox NDR200 Infiniband.
* These nodes are running [Rocky Linux 9](https://rockylinux.org/), which is a rebuild of Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9 (RHEL9).

## Recommendations for running on Sapphire

# Sapphire Rapid Nodes[¶](#sapphire-rapid-nodes "Permalink to this headline")

*These new nodes entered general service in July 2023.*

## Slurm partitions[¶](#slurm-partitions "Permalink to this headline")

* The Sapphire Rapid nodes are named according to the scheme *cpu-r-[1-112]*.
* Some of the Sapphire Rapid nodes are in the **sapphire** Slurm partition. Your existing
  -CPU projects will be able to submit jobs to these.
* The Sapphire Rapid nodes have **112 cpus** (1 cpu = 1 core), and **4580 MiB RAM per cpu** for a total of 512 GB RAM per node.
* The Sapphire Rapid nodes are interconnected by Mellanox NDR200 Infiniband
* The Sapphire Rapid nodes are running [Rocky Linux 8](https://rockylinux.org/), which is a rebuild of Red Hat Enterprise Linux 8 (RHEL8).

## Recommendations for running on Sapphire Rapids[¶](#recommendations-for-running-on-sapphire-rapids "Permalink to this headline")

Since the cpu-r nodes are running Rocky 8, you may need to recompile
your applications.

It is possible to log into a login node with compatible OS/software by logging into *login-icelake.hpc.cam.ac.uk*,
which will land you on one of the *login-q-\** nodes.

Alternatively, request an interactive node using *sintr*:

```
sintr -t 4:0:0 -N1 -n38 -A YOURPROJECT-CPU -p sapphire
```

The per-job wallclock time limits are 36 hours and 12 hours for SL1/2 and SL3
respectively.

The per-job, per-user cpu limits are now 4256 and 448 cpus for SL1/2
and SL3 respectively.

These limits should be regarded as provisional and may be revised.

### Default submission script for Sapphire

[TICKET_SUMMARY] Memory and network details

[INITIAL_DESCRIPTION]
Hello HPC support,
I am attaching a couple of files regarding  memory and  network  details in a cluster provided by Greg from your team.
 
May I confirm with you that the memory architecture output details 16 CHANNELS (I am based this info due to the Memory Device number)? 
I am quite confused because the file only shows Rank 2 instead of 16.
I was also checking the size and speed up to generalize how faster the movement "locally" is, in a node of a cluster. 
This information is not shown in all the Devices, the firmware nor the fabric are shown in detail either. 
My goal is to determine what kind of memory is used as well as its architecture in detail.
In case you can help me, it would be more than great! 
 
For the network case, I also want to confirm if there are two channels, and I want to determine the speed of each one. 
It is only possible to determine it with the date 2006 and standard InfiniBand rate? 
My goal here is to determine what kind of network is used as well as its architecture in detail. 
To determine which one is faster, the local memory flow or the intranode in the cluster with infiniband.
 
Thanks in advance for your time.
Julita Inca

```

The per-job wallclock time limits are 36 hours and 12 hours for SL1/2 and SL3
respectively.

The per-job, per-user cpu limits are now 4256 and 448 cpus for SL1/2
and SL3

metrics.reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: SRCP Access / Citrix Client Troubleshooting
Technical Action Required: No (diagnostic guidance)
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
User on VPN is unable to log in via the Citrix Workspace app. The appropriate first diagnostic is to verify whether browser-based launch through `anywhere.cam.ac.uk` works and whether the SRCP desktop can be started there, which helps separate a Citrix client problem from a broader access issue.

ACTION
1) Ask the user to test web launch via `https://anywhere.cam.ac.uk/`.
2) Verify whether the SRCP desktop is visible there and whether launching from the browser hands off to Citrix correctly.
3) If browser launch works, focus on the local Citrix Workspace client configuration or version.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Dear Daniel,

While connected to VPN, please log in via:
https://anywhere.cam.ac.uk/

Do you see your SRCP virtual desktop there, and does launching from the browser open Citrix correctly?

Regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Do not assume Citrix backend fault without basic path validation.
metrics.response:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Citrix Workspace / Login Issues [1][4]
Technical Action Required: Yes [1][4]
Portal Action Required: No [1][4]
Urgency: Medium [1][4]

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user is experiencing issues with logging into Citrix Workspace, specifically with the SRCP Secure Workspace [1][4]. The error message is not explicitly stated, but similar issues have been resolved by ensuring the Citrix desktop app is installed and launch options are set correctly [4], or by resetting the Citrix program [1].

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
1. Request the user to confirm if they have the Citrix desktop app installed and if the launch options are set correctly [4].
2. Ask the user to try resetting the Citrix program by following the instructions in [1].
3. If the issue persists, request the user to provide a screenshot of the error message for further investigation.

QUESTIONS TO ASK (ONLY IF NEEDED)
- Can you please provide a screenshot of the error message you are seeing?
- Have you tried resetting the Citrix program as described in [1]?
- Are you connected to the Cambridge VPN while trying to access Citrix Workspace [4]?

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello,
Thank you for reaching out. To troubleshoot the issue with Citrix Workspace, could you please try resetting the Citrix program by following the instructions in [1]? If the issue persists, please provide a screenshot of the error message for further investigation.
Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Ensure that the user is connected to the Cambridge VPN while trying to access Citrix Workspace [4].
- Do not attempt to troubleshoot the issue without verifying the user's setup and configuration [1][4].

REFERENCE KEY
[1] : HPCSSUP-99950
[4] : HPCSSUP-94787

## Frequently asked questions (FAQ)[¶](#frequently-asked-questions-faq "Permalink to this headline")

* I am unable to login to multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk and now I get “Connection refused”.

  > + Make sure you are using the same userid, password or public key as you would to login.hpc.cam.ac.uk. As with the login nodes, failing to authenticate three times in succession to multi will cause your address to be blocked for 20 minutes, so if this happens please wait this long before trying again.
* I am using MS Authenticator but the TOTP code doesn’t work.

  > + If you still have the QR code and secret key on your screen then delete the account in your Authenticator and recreate it, making sure that the account is of the **Other** type (not *Personal* or *Work*). Otherwise please contact *support@hpc.cam.ac.uk* to request reset of the MFA secret before trying again, and select account type **Other**.
* When performing ssh to multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk the QR code is replaced either by garbage or is broken up by blank lines, making it unusable.

  > + Setting **export LANG=en\_GB.UTF-8** in Mac terminals before performing the ssh may stop this happening - however if this has already happened you will need to contact *support@hpc.cam.ac.uk* to request reset of the MFA secret before trying again.
* Connection sharing doesn’t work with Filezilla and I keep seeing TOTP requests during file transfers.

## Frequently asked questions (FAQ)[¶](#frequently-asked-questions-faq "Permalink to this headline")

* I am unable to login to multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk and now I get “Connection refused”.

  > + Make sure you are using the same userid, password or public key as you would to login.hpc.cam.ac.uk. As with the login nodes, failing to authenticate three times in succession to multi will cause your address to be blocked for 20 minutes, so if this happens please wait this long before trying again.
* I am using MS Authenticator but the TOTP code doesn’t work.

  > + If you still have the QR code and secret key on your screen then delete the account in your Authenticator and recreate it, making sure that the account is of the **Other** type (not *Personal* or *Work*). Otherwise please contact *support@hpc.cam.ac.uk* to request reset of the MFA secret before trying again, and select account type **Other**.
* When performing ssh to multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk the QR code is replaced either by garbage or is broken up by blank lines, making it unusable.

  > + Setting **export LANG=en\_GB.UTF-8** in Mac terminals before performing the ssh may stop this happening - however if this has already happened you will need to contact *support@hpc.cam.ac.uk* to request reset of the MFA secret before trying again.
* Connection sharing doesn’t work with Filezilla and I keep seeing TOTP requests during file transfers.

  > + Please prefer to use the RDS gateway *rds.uis.cam.ac.uk* for file transfers. This will request TOTP less frequently.

User added a DiRAC project in SAFE but it is not yet visible in `mybalance`. Provisioning is not immediate, so the next step is to check whether the account association is still pending and complete the internal provisioning workflow if required.

CLASSIFICATION
Category: Storage / Project Management [1]
Technical Action Required: Yes [1]
Escalation Required: Possibly [1]
Urgency: Medium [1]

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user has been added to a new DiRAC project (DP371) via SAFE but the resources are not showing up as available on CSD3/Wilkes, and 'mybalance' does not show the project [1]. This issue is similar to previous cases where users were not able to see their projects after being added to them in SAFE [2][3].

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
To resolve this issue, the user needs to be added to the DP371 project and the corresponding RDS group [5]. The command `./t2-add-user-to-group rab200 dp371` needs to be run to add the user to the project and RDS group [5]. After this is done, the user should be able to see the project and its resources when running `mybalance` [4].

QUESTIONS TO ASSESS CURRENT STATE
- Can you confirm that you have requested to join the DP371 project through SAFE and that the request has been approved?
- Have you tried running `mybalance` after being added to the project to see if the project is listed?

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello,
To resolve the issue with not being able to see the DP371 project, we will need to add you to the project and the corresponding RDS group. We will run the command `./t2-add-user-to-group rab200 dp371` to add you to the project. After this is done, please try running `mybalance` again to see if the project is listed. If you have any further issues, please let us know.
Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Users need to be added to projects and RDS groups to access project resources [1][5].
- The command `./t2-add-user-to-group` is used to add users to projects and RDS groups [5].

mybalance are the projects you may specify in SLURM submissions either through

```
#SBATCH -A project
```

in the job submission script or equivalently on the command line with

```
sbatch -A project ...
```

Where -CPU projects should be used for CPU jobs and -GPU projects for GPU. See the [Submitting jobs](#submitting-jobs) section for details on submitting to each cluster.

**How many core hours does some other project or user have?**

```
gbalance -p T2BENCH-SL2-CPU
User           Usage | Account Usage         | Account Limit Available (hours)
---------- --------- + -------------- ------ + ------------- ---------
xyz10              0 | T2BENCH-SL2-CPU     0 | 200,000          200,000
```

This outputs the total usage in core hours accumulated to date for the project, the total awarded and total remaining available (i.e. to all members). It also prints the component of the total usage due to each member.

**I would like a listing of all jobs I have submitted through a certain project and between certain times**

```
gstatement -p SUPPORT-CPU -u xyz10 -s "2023-09-06-00:00:00" -e "2023-09-06-23:59:59"

# Running Jobs on CSD3[¶](#running-jobs-on-csd3 "Permalink to this headline")

SLURM is an open source workload management and job scheduling system. Research Computing clusters adopted SLURM in February 2014, but previously used Torque, Maui/Moab and Gold (referred to in the following simply as “PBS”) for the same purpose. Please note that there are several commands available in SLURM with the same names as in PBS (e.g. showq, qstat, qsub, qdel, qrerun) intended for backwards compatibility, but in general we would recommend using the native SLURM commands, as described below.

If you have any questions on how to run jobs on CSD3 do not hesitate
to contact the support desk.

mybalance are the projects you may specify in SLURM submissions either through

```
#SBATCH -A project
```

in the job submission script or equivalently on the command line with

```
sbatch -A project ...
```

Where -CPU projects should be used for CPU jobs and -GPU projects for GPU. See the [Submitting jobs](#submitting-jobs) section for details on submitting to each cluster.

**How many core hours does some other project or user have?**

```
gbalance -p T2BENCH-SL2-CPU
User           Usage | Account Usage         | Account Limit Available (hours)
---------- --------- + -------------- ------ + ------------- ---------
xyz10              0 | T2BENCH-SL2-CPU     0 | 200,000          200,000
```

This outputs the total usage in core hours accumulated to date for the project, the total awarded and total remaining available (i.e. to all members). It also prints the component of the total usage due to each member.

# Running Jobs on CSD3[¶](#running-jobs-on-csd3 "Permalink to this headline")

SLURM is an open source workload management and job scheduling system. Research Computing clusters adopted SLURM in February 2014, but previously used Torque, Maui/Moab and Gold (referred to in the following simply as “PBS”) for the same purpose. Please note that there are several commands available in SLURM with the same names as in PBS (e.g. showq, qstat, qsub, qdel, qrerun) intended for backwards compatibility, but in general we would recommend using the native SLURM commands, as described below.

If you have any questions on how to run jobs on CSD3 do not hesitate
to contact the support desk.

## Accounting Commands[¶](#accounting-commands "Permalink to this headline")

**What resources do I have available to me?**

mybalance

* on CPU nodes we are allocating and reporting in **CPU core hours**
* on GPU nodes we are allocating and reporting in **GPU hours**.

We have adopted the convention that projects containing CPU hours for use on CPU nodes will end in -CPU, while those holding GPU hours for use on GPU nodes end in -GPU.

metrics.reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Software Environment / Dawn PVC / PyTorch-IPEX
Technical Action Required: No (user setup guidance)
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
User wants a modifiable local environment equivalent to centrally managed `pytorch-gpu`. Central env is immutable. Supported options:
- activate central `pytorch-gpu` then layer user virtualenv, or
- build user conda env with compatible pinned versions from Intel channel and test on PVC nodes.

ACTION
1) Confirm central env baseline works.
2) Explain central env cannot be modified.
3) Provide two supported approaches:
   - `pytorch-gpu` + user virtualenv layering
   - standalone conda env with compatible pins (`intel-extension-for-pytorch`, `pytorch`, python range) and Intel repo
4) Require testing on PVC/PVC9 interactive node.
5) Set required env vars for XPU runtime test.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Hi Moritz,

`pytorch-gpu` is centrally managed and cannot be modified directly.

Supported options are:
1) Activate `pytorch-gpu`, then activate your own virtualenv for extra packages.
2) Create your own conda env with compatible versions (e.g. pinned `intel-extension-for-pytorch` + `pytorch`) and test it on PVC nodes.

Please use the Dawn guidance here:
https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/pvc.html#machine-learning-data-science-frameworks

Best wishes,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Do not advise modifying centrally managed environments.
metrics.response:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Software / PyTorch Environment Management
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: Possibly
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user is trying to create a local Conda environment compatible with the Intel GPU setup used on DAWN, and ensure the installation of all necessary packages without running into access issues [1][2]. The user has attempted to clone the pytorch-gpu environment and install all its modules manually but encountered HTTP 403 errors [1].

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
To create a clone of the pytorch-gpu environment, the user can try the following steps:
1. Load the necessary modules: `module purge`, `module load rhel9/default-dawn`, `module load intelpython-conda/2025.0` [3].
2. Activate the pytorch-gpu environment: `conda activate pytorch-gpu-2.3.1` [3].
3. Create a clone of the environment: `conda create --yes --prefix "${FULL_VENV_NAME}" --clone pytorch-gpu-2.3.1` [3].
If the user encounters issues with installing packages, they can try loading the intel-oneapi-mkl module: `module load intel-oneapi-mkl/2025.1.0/oneapi/hc67rita` [3].

QUESTIONS TO ASK (ONLY IF NEEDED)
- Can you provide more details about the HTTP 403 errors you encountered?
- Have you tried loading the intel-oneapi-mkl module before installing packages?

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello,
To create a local Conda environment compatible with the Intel GPU setup used on DAWN, you can try the following steps:
1. Load the necessary modules: `module purge`, `module load rhel9/default-dawn`, `module load intelpython-conda/2025.0`.
2. Activate the pytorch-gpu environment: `conda activate pytorch-gpu-2.3.1`.
3. Create a clone of the environment: `conda create --yes --prefix "${FULL_VENV_NAME}" --clone pytorch-gpu-2.3.1`.
If you encounter issues with installing packages, you can try loading the intel-oneapi-mkl module: `module load intel-oneapi-mkl/2025.1.0/oneapi/hc67rita`.
Please let us know if you have any further questions or issues.
Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Be cautious when creating and managing Conda environments to avoid conflicts with system-provided environments [1][2].
- Ensure that you have the necessary permissions and access to create and manage Conda environments [1][2].

REFERENCE KEY
[1] : HPCSSUP-75523
[2] : https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/pvc-jobs-ML.html::chunk::0000
[3] : HPCSSUP-91281
metrics.user_input:
Dear HPC Team,
I am currently using the DAWN cluster and would like to leverage the Intel GPUs for running my PyTorch code.
According to the documentation, the following setup works well:
module load intelpython-conda
conda activate pytorch-gpu
And in Python:
import torch
import intel_extension_for_pytorch as ipex
...
1. Enable GPU
model = model.to('xpu')
data = data.to('xpu')
model = ipex.optimize(model, dtype=torch.float32)
This setup functions as expected.
However, the pytorch-gpu environment appears to be a system-provided environment. For the purposes of my project, I would like to create and manage my own local Conda environment that is compatible with the Intel GPUs (i.e., includes PyTorch and the Intel Extension for PyTorch with xpu support).
I have attempted to clone the pytorch-gpu environment as well as install all of its modules manually, but I run into repeated HTTP 403 errors when trying to fetch packages from the Intel Conda channel. This seems to indicate restricted access or deprecation of some packages or channels.
Could you please advise on the recommended way to:
1.  Create a local Conda environment compatible with the Intel GPU setup used on DAWN,
2.  Ensure installation of all necessary packages without running into access issues?
If there is a supported method to export or replicate the pytorch-gpu environment into a user-managed Conda environment, that would be ideal.
Thank you very much for your assistance!
Best wishes,
Moritz

# Dawn - Machine Learning & Data Science frameworks[¶](#dawn-machine-learning-data-science-frameworks "Permalink to this headline")

We provide a set of pre-populated Conda environments based on the Intel
Distribution for Python:

```
module av intelpython-conda
conda info -e
```

This module provides environments for PyTorch and Tensorflow.

Please note that Intel code and documentation sometimes refers to ‘XPUs’, a more
generic term for accelerators, GPU or otherwise. For Dawn, ‘XPU’ and ‘GPU’ can
usually be considered interchangeable.

## PyTorch[¶](#pytorch "Permalink to this headline")

PyTorch on Intel GPUs is supported by the [Intel Extension for PyTorch](https://intel.github.io/intel-extension-for-pytorch/xpu/latest/).
On Dawn this version of PyTorch is accessible as a conda environment named pytorch-gpu:

```
module load intelpython-conda
conda activate pytorch-gpu
```

Adapting your code to run on the PVCs is straightforward and only takes a few
lines of code. For details, see the official documentation - but as a quick
example:

```
import torch
import intel_extension_for_pytorch as ipex

...

# Enable GPU
model = model.to('xpu')
data = data.to('xpu')

model = ipex.optimize(model, dtype=torch.float32)
```

## TensorFlow[¶](#tensorflow "Permalink to this headline")

Intel supports optimised TensorFlow on both CPU and GPU, using the [Intel
Extension for TensorFlow](https://intel.github.io/intel-extension-for-tensorflow/latest/get_started.html).
On Dawn this version on TensorFlow is accessible as a conda environment named tensorflow-gpu:

```
module load

# PyTorch[¶](#pytorch "Permalink to this headline")

The PyTorch [website](https://pytorch.org) describes the library as:

> Tensors and Dynamic neural networks in Python with strong GPU acceleration.

## Setup PyTorch on CSD3[¶](#setup-pytorch-on-csd3 "Permalink to this headline")

The python installation will depend on the target partition (CPU or GPU).
To run Pytorch on a CPU partition (cclake, icelake), please do the following:

```
#!/bin/bash

# load the relevant cluster environment and a recent python built for the cluster:
module purge
module load rhel8/default-ccl # recommended for all RHEL8 CPU partitions (cclake, icelake, sapphire)
module load python/3.11.9/gcc/nptrdpll

# create and activate a virtual environment:

python -m venv ./pytorch-cclake-env
source ./pytorch-cclake-env/bin/activate

# install pytorch into your virtual env (instructions from Intel: https://pytorch-extension.intel.com/installation?platform=cpu&version=v2.7.0%2Bcpu&os=linux%2Fwsl2&package=pip)
python -m pip install torch torchvision torchaudio --index-url https://download.pytorch.org/whl/cpu
python -m pip install intel-extension-for-pytorch
python -m pip install oneccl_bind_pt --extra-index-url https://pytorch-extension.intel.com/release-whl/stable/cpu/us/
```

To run Pytorch on a GPU partition (ampere), please follow the following instructions:

```
#!/bin/bash

# load the relevant cluster environment and a recent python built for the cluster:
module purge
module load rhel8/default-amp
module load python/3.8.11/gcc-9.4.0-yb6rzr6

# create and activate a virtual environment:
python -m

CLASSIFICATION
Category: Storage Path Discovery
Technical Action Required: No
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Low

QUICK ASSESSMENT
User could not locate AIRR/Dawn storage path in docs. The exact project path is user/project-specific and should be discovered from the user account rather than assumed.

ACTION
1) Ask user to check available RDS links under home:
   - `ls -ld ~/rds/*`
2) Ask user to identify the AIRR project path from quota/project listing:
   - `quota`
3) Confirm the matching path format once identified (typically `~/rds/rds-<project-id>` symlink).
4) Clarify that `~/rds/...` is a convenience link to the underlying project path.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Dear Phil,

Your AIRR storage path is user/project specific. Please run:

`ls -ld ~/rds/*`
`quota`

This will show the project link available in your account (typically `~/rds/rds-<project-id>`).  
That entry is a symlink to the underlying project storage location.

Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Do not hardcode project-specific paths unless verified for that user/account.

You have two choices for your identity provider:

* University Login (MyAccessID)
* Other Login (Identity of Last Resort)

Make sure to select the option that corresponds to the email address where you received your invitation.

If your invitation was sent to your university account, choose the “Sign in with Keycloak” option. This will allow you to log in using your University credentials.

# Dawn - Intel GPU (PVC) Nodes[¶](#dawn-intel-gpu-pvc-nodes "Permalink to this headline")

*These new nodes entered Early Access service in January 2024*

[![Banner image for the Dawn supercomputer - The word Dawn in orange lettering over a nighttime satellite image of the UK and Europe with the sun beginning to rise.](../_images/dawn-banner.jpg)](../_images/dawn-banner.jpg)

## Dawn Documentation[¶](#dawn-documentation "Permalink to this headline")

## **AIRR**

**Welcome to Cambridge Research Computing Access Portal**

The Cambridge Research Computing Access Portal is a web-based platform developed by the University of Cambridge to provide secure, streamlined access to DAWN, one of the UK’s national AI Research Resource (AIRR) systems. It operates under the broader UKRI-supported AI Research Resource Federation (AIRRFED) initiative. A parallel facility, Isambard-AI, is hosted at the University of Bristol, forming the other half of the national AIRR infrastructure.

The portal enables researchers to:

* Request access to DAWN’s compute resources,
* Manage project membership and roles, and
* View and report on usage statistics.

This documentation provides a comprehensive guide to navigating the system, understanding its features, and utilizing its full capabilities.

**Overview of AIRRFED**

AIRRFED aims to provide secure, federated access to national AI and HPC infrastructures. This project is a collaborative effort involving multiple institutions, with Dawn at the University of Cambridge and Isambard-AI at the University of Bristol serving as key sites.

Authentication

Technical Action Required: No (explanation + minor script advice)
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Low

QUICK ASSESSMENT
User saw scheduler reason text and assumed there was a post-maintenance fault. From the script provided, there is no obvious submission syntax problem, and this kind of reason string can still appear while a job is simply pending under normal scheduler priority. Additional optimisation: explicit `--mem` is redundant for cclake in this case.

ACTION
1) Check live job state and reason (`squeue`, `scontrol`).
2) Explain pending-by-priority behavior.
3) Suggest removing redundant `--mem` directive when CPU request already implies desired memory.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Dear Hamish,

From the script you sent, the job appears to have submitted correctly and may simply be pending under normal scheduler priority.

For this script, `--mem=27360M` is redundant if you already request the corresponding CPU count on `cclake`; removing it can simplify scheduling requests.

Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Distinguish temporary reason strings from real submission errors.

CLASSIFICATION
Category: Job Submission / Queue Management [1]
Technical Action Required: Possibly [1]
Portal Action Required: No [2]
Urgency: Medium [1]

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The job is pending with a status of "ReqNodeNotAvail, Reserved for maintenance" [1], despite maintenance having finished last week. This issue has been seen before in similar cases [3][5], where temporary measures or maintenance were taken, affecting node availability.

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
Check the current status of the cclake partition and nodes [2]. Investigate if there are any ongoing or scheduled maintenance activities that may be causing the issue [1][5]. If the issue persists, consider resubmitting the job to a different partition, such as icelake, if suitable for the job requirements [2].

QUESTIONS TO ASK (ONLY IF NEEDED)
- Are there any error messages or warnings in the job output or logs?
- Has the job been resubmitted or modified since the initial submission?
- Are there any specific requirements or constraints for the job that may be contributing to the issue?

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello Hamish,
Thank you for reaching out. We are investigating the issue with your job pending in the queue. In the meantime, please check if resubmitting the job to a different partition, such as icelake, is suitable for your job requirements [2]. If you have any further questions or concerns, please don't hesitate to ask.
Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Be cautious when resubmitting jobs to different partitions, as it may affect job performance or resource allocation [2].
- Always check the current status of the partition and nodes before submitting a job [1].

metrics.user_input:
Hi -
I’ve just sbatched a job on the HPC, using cclake, job 9082481_[1-2]. If I squeue -u has27 it tells me (under NODELIST) 'ReqNodeNotAvail, Reserved for maintenance’. But maintenance finished last week.
Can you tell me what (if anything) I’ve done wrong? Script below.
Thanks,
Hamish
#!/bin/bash
#SBATCH -A GLOVER-SL3-CPU
#SBATCH --nodes=1
#SBATCH --mail-type=NONE
#SBATCH -p cclake  # name of the partition to run job on
#SBATCH -o /home/has27/rds/hpc-work/f2reads/bowtie2/bowtie2%a.log
#SBATCH -c 8         # number of CPUs. Default: 1
#SBATCH --mem=27360M     # RAM memory.
#SBATCH -t 12:00:00  # time for the job HH:MM:SS. Default: 1 min
#SBATCH -a 1-2
source ~/.bashrc
1. activate conda environment
conda activate bowtie2
cd /home/has27/rds/hpc-work/f2reads/soapnuke/clean
1. List all files in the folder starting with A or B,
2. then get the relevant line
INDIV_ID=$(ls -d [AB]* | head -n ${SLURM_ARRAY_TASK_ID} | tail -n 1)
cd $INDIV_ID
READ1=$(ls *1.fq.gz)
READ2=$(ls *2.fq.gz)
cd /home/has27/rds/hpc-work/Genomes/LSRagtag
bowtie2 -p 8 -x LSRagtag -1 /home/has27/rds/hpc-work/f2reads/soapnuke/clean/$INDIV_ID/$READ1 -2 /home/has27/rds/hpc-work/f2reads/soapnuke/clean/$INDIV_ID/$READ2 | samtools sort > /home/has27/rds/hpc-work/f2reads/align-LSRagtag/$INDIV_ID.sorted.bam
—
Dr Hamish Symington
Junior Research Fellow, Queens’ College, Cambridge
Academic Skills Tutor and Bye-Fellow, Downing College, Cambridge
Postdoctoral Researcher in the Glover Lab, Department of Plant Sciences, University of Cambridge

JOBID PARTITION     NAME     USER ST          START_TIME  NODES SCHEDNODES           NODELIST(REASON)

partition directive, i.e.

```
#SBATCH -p icelake
```

for 3.4GB per CPU nodes, and

```
#SBATCH -p icelake-himem
```

for 6.8GB per CPU nodes. Jobs requesting more than 3.4GB per CPU may still be submitted to the icelake partition, but may be automatically assigned extra CPUs to cover the additional memory, and will therefore be charged more usage credits. Therefore it is recommended that whenever possible jobs requiring more than 3.4GB per CPU be submitted to icelake-himem.

The cclake and cclake-himem partitions work in similar ways but the exact numbers vary according to the node hardware - see the template job submission scripts for details.

The Wilkes3-GPU (ampere) nodes each contain 4 NVIDIA A100 GPUs. It is possible to request 1, 2, 3, or 4 GPUs for a single node job, but multinode jobs will need to request 4 GPUs per node, which would be done by using the directives

```
#SBATCH -p ampere
#SBATCH --gres=gpu:4
```

(see `/usr/local/Cluster-Docs/SLURM/slurm_submit.wilkes3`).

The job’s status in the queue can be monitored with *squeue*; alternatively use *qstat* or *showq* (add -u username to focus on a particular user’s jobs).

The job can be deleted with *scancel <job\_id>* or *qdel <job\_id>*.

When the job finishes (in error or correctly) there will normally be one file created in the submission directory with a name of the form slurm-NNNN.out (where NNNN is the job id).

## Problems[¶](#problems "Permalink to this headline")

Please check first of all whether there is an answer to your question in the FAQ. If not, please request support with your details and

long time are prepared to let others do so too while waiting for a start time.

QOSL is implemented by two SLURM QoS definitions: icelake-long and cclake-long have `cpul`, which is restricted to 512 cpus per user; ampere-long has `gpul`, which is restricted to 32 GPUs per user.

In order to apply for access to QOSL, please email support@hpc.cam.ac.uk detailing why this mode of usage is necessary, and explaining why checkpointing is not a practical option for your application.

## Submitting long jobs[¶](#submitting-long-jobs "Permalink to this headline")

Use of QOSL is tied to the -long partitions, therefore once given access it is necessary only to specify this partition - e.g.

```
sbatch -t 7-0:0:0 -p icelake-long -A YOUR_PROJECT ...
```

[INITIAL_DESCRIPTION]
Good morning,

(ReqNodeNotAvail, Reserved for maintenance)

the queueing system:

```
/usr/local/Cluster-Docs/SLURM/slurm_submit.peta4-cclake

/usr/local/Cluster-Docs/SLURM/slurm_submit.peta4-icelake

/usr/local/Cluster-Docs/SLURM/slurm_submit.wilkes3
```

Copying the appropriate example file and then modifying the top section (where indicated) will create a script suitable for submission to the batch queueing system via the command sbatch.

There are also examples of submission scripts for specific applications in this documentation - see for example [CASTEP](/hpc/software-packages/castep.html) or [LAMMPS](/hpc/software-packages/lammps.html).

Peta4 and Wilkes3 operate the SLURM batch queueing system for managing resources. Some useful commands:

```
squeue      -> show global cluster information
sinfo       -> show global cluster information
sview       -> show global cluster information
scontrol show job nnnn -> examine the job with jobid nnnn
scontrol show node nodename -> examine the node with name nodename
sbatch      -> submits an executable script to the queueing system
sintr       -> submits an interactive job to the queueing system
srun        -> run a command either as a new job or within an existing job
scancel     -> delete a job
mybalance   -> show current balance of core hour credits
```

Once your application is compiled, e.g. to a binary called *prog*, it can be submitted to the queueing system as follows (we assume it is destined for Ice Lake).

Firstly, copy the template SLURM submission script:

```
cp /usr/local/Cluster-Docs/SLURM/slurm_submit.peta4-icelake slurm_submit
```

Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
Mac terminal rendering can distort SSH QR code during MFA enrollment. Enrollment can still proceed by scanning if possible, or manually entering the secret key in authenticator app.

ACTION
1) Confirm issue is known for some Mac terminal setups.
2) Provide manual secret-key fallback method.
3) Clarify expected token labels for SSH vs web.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Hi James,

This is a known issue on some Mac setups where the QR may render poorly. You can still complete setup by manually entering the secret key in your authenticator app if scanning fails.

Please follow:
https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/mfa.html

The SSH token should appear as `CSD3: SSH Login` (distinct from login-web token naming).

Best wishes,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Do not reset MFA unnecessarily when manual enrollment path is available.

CLASSIFICATION

Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: Possibly
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user is experiencing an issue with the QR code being replaced by garbage or broken up by blank lines when attempting to set up MFA on multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk [2]. This is a known issue, and the solution involves resetting the MFA secret [2][4].

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
To resolve the issue, we need to reset the MFA secret for the user's account. This requires verifying the user's identity through a photo ID, which can be done via an MS Teams call [4]. Once the identity is verified, we can reset the MFA secret and provide the user with a new QR code or secret key to set up their TOTP [2][4].

QUESTIONS TO ASK (ONLY IF NEEDED)
- Can you please confirm your availability for an MS Teams call to verify your identity?
- Have you tried setting up the TOTP using the secret key instead of the QR code [2][4]?

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello,
We can assist with resetting your MFA secret to resolve the issue with the QR code. To proceed, we need to verify your identity through a photo ID via an MS Teams call. Please let us know a convenient time for the call.
Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Resetting the MFA secret requires verifying the user's identity to ensure account security [4].
- Users should keep their TOTP secret keys and emergency one-time passcodes secure to prevent unauthorized access [3].

* I am unable to login to multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk and now I get “Connection refused”.

  > + Make sure you are using the same userid, password or public key as you would to login.hpc.cam.ac.uk. As with the login nodes, failing to authenticate three times in succession to multi will cause your address to be blocked for 20 minutes, so if this happens please wait this long before trying again.
* I am using MS Authenticator but the TOTP code doesn’t work.

  > + If you still have the QR code and secret key on your screen then delete the account in your Authenticator and recreate it, making sure that the account is of the **Other** type (not *Personal* or *Work*). Otherwise please contact *support@hpc.cam.ac.uk* to request reset of the MFA secret before trying again, and select account type **Other**.
* When performing ssh to multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk the QR code is replaced either by garbage or is broken up by blank lines, making it unusable.

* Connection sharing doesn’t work with Filezilla and I keep seeing TOTP requests during file transfers.

  > + Please prefer to use the RDS gateway *rds.uis.cam.ac.uk* for file transfers. This will request TOTP less frequently.

Dear HPC support,
I hope this email finds you well.

continue connecting (yes/no/[fingerprint])?
```

### Step 2: Log into multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk[¶](#step-2-log-into-multi-hpc-cam-ac-uk "Permalink to this headline")

Once you have connected, you may see a password prompt to log in.

![multi login - access](../_images/multi-login.png)

**University of Cambridge users**: You will need to enter your University password.

**External users**: You will need to use your SSH key or your password, depending on how your account was created.

If TOTP is already configured for your account, a message will be printed and the connection closed:

![multi login - TOTP already configured](../_images/multi-noop.png)

Otherwise, a QR code and secret key will be printed.

![multi login - QR generated](../_images/multi-qr.png)

*Please be careful not to point your camera at the example QR code displayed on the web page, as it will happily read it but this code is not valid for any purpose. You want to use* **your** *QR code from* **your** *login session to multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk.*

### Step 3: Scan the QR code or enter the secret key.[¶](#step-3-scan-the-qr-code-or-enter-the-secret-key "Permalink to this headline")

In your chosen authenticator app, you will want to create a new entry for your CSD3 SSH Login TOTP. In this walkthrough, we use Google Authenticator and Microsoft Authenticator as examples.

Both Google Authenticator or Microsoft Authenticator display a ‘+’ icon for adding a new account:

> * **Google Authenticator** then presents a choice of *Scan a QR code* or *Enter a setup key*, i.e. either point your phone camera at the displayed QR

### Step 2: Log into multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk[¶](#step-2-log-into-multi-hpc-cam-ac-uk "Permalink to this headline")

Once you have connected, you may see a password prompt to log in.

![multi login - access](../_images/multi-login.png)

**University of Cambridge users**: You will need to enter your University password.

**External users**: You will need to use your SSH key or your password, depending on how your account was created.

If TOTP is already configured for your account, a message will be printed and the connection closed:

![multi login - TOTP already configured](../_images/multi-noop.png)

Otherwise, a QR code and secret key will be printed.

![multi login - QR generated](../_images/multi-qr.png)

*Please be careful not to point your camera at the example QR code displayed on the web page, as it will happily read it but this code is not valid for any purpose. You want to use* **your** *QR code from* **your** *login session to multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk.*

### Step 3: Scan the QR code or enter the secret key.[¶](#step-3-scan-the-qr-code-or-enter-the-secret-key "Permalink to this headline")

In your chosen authenticator app, you will want to create a new entry for your CSD3 SSH Login TOTP. In this walkthrough, we use Google Authenticator and Microsoft Authenticator as examples.

Both Google Authenticator or Microsoft Authenticator display a ‘+’ icon for adding a new account:

> * **Google Authenticator** then presents a choice of *Scan a QR code* or *Enter a setup key*, i.e. either point your phone camera at the displayed QR

CLASSIFICATION
Category: Cloud VM Configuration / GPU Containers / MPI
Technical Action Required: No (guidance)
Escalation Required: Optional (if capacity constrained)
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
User needed guidance for CUDA+MPI in Azimuth GPU VM. Recommended approach: install CUDA toolkit and Nvidia driver in VM, prefer Singularity for HPC container runtime with GPU bindings, and choose MPI runtime inside container as needed. Capacity caveat: GPU pool may be fully utilised.

ACTION
1) Confirm current GPU capacity availability.
2) Advise VM-level CUDA/driver install for GPU instances.
3) Recommend Singularity/Apptainer over Docker for HPC runtime.
4) Explain MPI runtime can be selected within container image.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Hello Bradley,

On Azimuth GPU VMs, you can install the CUDA toolkit and NVIDIA open-source driver in the usual way. For HPC workflows we generally recommend Singularity/Apptainer rather than Docker, including GPU bindings to expose host GPUs.

MPI runtime selection is up to your container build choice.

Please note GPU capacity may currently be fully allocated, so provisioning can be constrained.

Thanks,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Do not imply immediate GPU availability when capacity is saturated.

metrics.reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Jupyter / Login-Web / User Environment
Technical Action Required: No
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Low

QUICK ASSESSMENT
User could not select a conda environment kernel in Login-Web Jupyter. Resolution is to ensure the environment has `ipykernel` and is explicitly registered as a Jupyter kernel, then restart/reopen the notebook session.

ACTION
1) Confirm environment prerequisites
   - Environment exists and can be activated.
   - `ipykernel` is installed in that environment.
2) Register the environment as a Jupyter kernel
   - Activate env and run:
     `python -m ipykernel install --user --name <env_name> --display-name "Python (<env_name>)"`
3) Ask user to restart/reopen Login-Web Jupyter session and select the new kernel.
4) If kernel still does not appear, collect diagnostics:
   - `jupyter kernelspec list`
   - exact env activation/install commands used.
5) Share official docs for full workflow/reference.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Hello Patrick,

Please register your conda environment as a Jupyter kernel, then restart/reopen your Login-Web Jupyter session.

Example:

```bash
conda activate <env_name>
python -m ipykernel install --user --name <env_name> --display-name "Python (<env_name>)"
```

After that, select `Python (<env_name>)` from the Jupyter kernel menu.

Full guidance:
https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/login-web.html#adding-virtual-environments

If it still doesn’t appear, please share:
- `jupyter kernelspec list`
- the exact commands you used to create and register the environment.

Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Keep instructions aligned with the documented Login-Web kernel registration workflow.
metrics.response:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Jupyter Notebook / Conda Environment
Technical Action Required: Yes
Portal Action Required: No
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user is having trouble activating a Conda environment within a Jupyter Notebook on the Login-Web interface [1][5]. The user has already created a Conda environment with ipykernel installed [1]. To use a Conda environment in Jupyter Notebook, the user needs to add the environment as a new kernel [2][3].

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
To activate the Conda environment in Jupyter Notebook, the user should follow these steps:
1. Use the `Remote Desktop` application to log to the CSD3 account, then start a `Terminal` [2][3].
2. Activate the Conda environment [2][3].
3. Install the ipykernel package using `pip install ipykernel` [2][3].
4. Add the Conda environment as a new kernel using `ipython kernel install --user --name=<conda_env_name>` [2][3].
5. Logout and restart the Jupyter Notebook session [2][3].

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello,
To activate your Conda environment in Jupyter Notebook, please follow the steps outlined in our documentation [2][3]. If you have already installed ipykernel and added the environment as a new kernel, try restarting your Jupyter Notebook session [2][3]. If you are still experiencing issues, please provide more details about the error message you are seeing.
Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- The user should follow the documented steps to add a Conda environment as a new kernel in Jupyter Notebook [2][3].
- The user should not attempt to activate the Conda environment within the Jupyter Notebook using `!conda activate` [1].

REFERENCE KEY
[1] : HPCSSUP-87352
[2] : https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/login-web.html::chunk::0008
[3] : https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/loginweb-apps-jupyter.html::chunk::0001
[5] : https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/loginweb-apps-jupyter.html::chunk::0000

[TICKET_SUMMARY] Trouble using Conda environment with Jupyter Notebook on HPC

[CONTEXT]
Hi Elisabeth,
My issue is not about creating the Jupyter session, this part is working just fine. What I'm having trouble trying to do is activating one of my conda environments within the notebook.
Could you help me with this issue ?
Regards,
Timothee

[CONVERSATION]
<MESSAGE id=0004 role=HELPDESK_ASSIGNEE>
Hello Timothee,
Have you installed the ipykernel into your conda environment? You need this to be installed into order to set the conda environment on your Jupyter notebook. To do this, you will need to run: `pip install ipykernel` once installed you will then need to run: `ipython kernel install --user --name=[conda_venv_name]`
Additionally to connect to a Juypter notebook session via terminal, you will need to specify a login node that you have access to, such as one of the login-p or login-q nodes.
Best regards,
Elisabeth

</MESSAGE><MESSAGE id=0005 role=TICKET_CREATOR>
Hi Elisabeth,
It is working fine after creating the kernel with the conda environment. Thank you very much!
Feel free to close the ticket.
Regards,
Timothée

#### Adding virtual environments[¶](#adding-virtual-environments "Permalink to this headline")

To use your own virtual environments in the Jupyter Notebook app, you need to add them externally. Use the following steps to do so:

1. Use the `Remote Desktop` application to log to your CSD3 account, then start a `Terminal`.
2. If needed, create a new virtual environment using the commands described in the section [Setup Jupyter on CSD3](../software-packages/jupyter.html#setup-jupyter-on-csd3)
3. **Activate the virtual environment** (otherwise, your installed packages won’t be registered)
4. Deploy the python module **ipykernel** with the command line:

   ```
   pip install ipykernel
   ```
5. Add your virtual environment to the Jupyter Notebook as new kernel, with the command line:

   ```
   ipython kernel install --user --name=<virtual environment name>
   ```
6. Logout.
7. Start or refresh your jupyter notebook session, as described in

# Jupyter Notebook[¶](#jupyter-notebook "Permalink to this headline")

The Jupyter Notebook is an open-source web application that allows you to create and share documents that contain live code, equations, visualisations and narrative text. Uses include: data cleaning and transformation, numerical simulation, statistical modeling, data visualisation, machine learning, and much more.

## Accessing through Login-Web[¶](#accessing-through-login-web "Permalink to this headline")

All the desktop apps can be found within the `Interactive Apps` dropdown in the Login-Web web portal. After selecting Jupyter, you will arrive at this page:

![Jupyter Notebook](../_images/ondemand-apps-jupyter-menu.png)

Here you can select the resources you would like your Jupyter session to have using the menu on the right hand side of the page. After you click `Launch`, a new session will be queued. Once the session is active your page will look like the image below:

![Jupyter Notebook](../_images/ondemand-apps-jupyter-launch.png)

You can choose `Connect to Jupyter` to start your session and create a `New` Python 3 notebook.

![Jupyter Notebook](../_images/ondemand-apps-jupyter-new.png)

This is a new notebook with a familiar interface:

![Jupyter Notebook](../_images/ondemand-apps-jupyter.png)

## Adding virtual environments[¶](#adding-virtual-environments "Permalink to this headline")

[TICKET_KEY] HPCSSUP-87352

[CREATED] 2025-04-29T14:25:19.706+0100
[SUMMARY] Trouble using Conda environment with Jupyter Notebook on HPC

[INITIAL_DESCRIPTION]
Dear HPC Support Team,
I’m currently trying to use a Conda environment through a Jupyter Notebook on the Cambridge HPC system, but I’m running into a couple of issues.
Firstly, when I launch the interactive Jupyter app and attempt to activate my environment using:
!conda activate conda_env
I receive the following error:
CommandNotFoundError: Your shell has not been properly configured to use 'conda activate'.
To initialize your shell, run
$ conda init <SHELL_NAME>
Currently supported shells are:
- bash
- fish
- tcsh
- xonsh
- zsh
- powershell
See 'conda init --help' for more information and options.
I tried sourcing the bash configuration file from the Miniconda folder used by the Jupyter kernel, but this did not resolve the issue.
Additionally, I followed the instructions from your documentation here: [https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/software-packages/jupyter.html](https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/software-packages/jupyter.html)
However, when I try to forward the Jupyter port using the command:
ssh -L 8081:127.0.0.1:8081 -fN login-e-1.hpc.cam.ac.uk
from my local machine, the connection is closed immediately after entering my credentials, without any error message. This happens even without the -fN options.

#### Adding virtual environments[¶](#adding-virtual-environments "Permalink to this headline")

To use your own virtual environments in the Jupyter Notebook app, you need to add them externally. Use the following steps to do so:

1. Use the `Remote Desktop` application to log to your CSD3 account, then start a `Terminal`.
2. If needed, create a new virtual environment using the commands described in the section [Setup Jupyter on CSD3](../software-packages/jupyter.html#setup-jupyter-on-csd3)
3. **Activate the virtual environment** (otherwise, your installed packages won’t be registered)
4. Deploy the python module **ipykernel** with the command line:

   ```
   pip install ipykernel
   ```
5. Add your virtual environment to the Jupyter Notebook as new kernel, with the command line:

   ```
   ipython kernel install --user --name=<virtual environment name>
   ```
6. Logout.
7. Start or refresh your jupyter notebook session, as described in

   ```
5. Add your virtual environment to the Jupyter Notebook as new kernel, with the command line:

   ```
   ipython kernel install --user --name=<virtual environment name>
   ```
6. Logout.
7. Start or refresh your jupyter notebook session, as described in the previous section [Accessing through Login-Web](#jupyter-access-loginweb)
8. Start a new logbook by clicking on the button “New” on the right-hand side:

![Jupyter Notebook](../_images/ondemand-apps-jupyter-envs.jpg)

Your virtual environment should be listed under the selected `Python3` as shown in the above illustration. Selecting the virtual environment as kernel will give you access to the python packages you have installed in the virtual environment.

![Jupyter Notebook](../_images/ondemand-apps-jupyter-with-virtual-env.png)

## Delisting virtual environments[¶](#delisting-virtual-environments "Permalink to this headline")

To remove unwanted kernels from the Jupyter notebook:

1. Use the `Remote Desktop` application to log to your CSD3 account, then start a `Terminal`.
2. At the prompt:

   ```
   module load jupyterlab/3
   jupyter kernelspec list
   ```
3. The last command should give you the same list of kernels as the

# Jupyter Notebook[¶](#jupyter-notebook "Permalink to this headline")

The Jupyter Notebook is an open-source web application that allows you to create and share documents that contain live code, equations, visualisations and narrative text. Uses include: data cleaning and transformation, numerical simulation, statistical modeling, data visualisation, machine learning, and much more.

## Accessing through Login-Web[¶](#accessing-through-login-web "Permalink to this headline")

All the desktop apps can be found within the `Interactive Apps` dropdown in the Login-Web web portal. After selecting Jupyter, you will arrive at this page:

![Jupyter Notebook](../_images/ondemand-apps-jupyter-menu.png)

Here you can select the resources you would like your Jupyter session to have using the menu on the right hand side of the page. After you click `Launch`, a new session will be queued. Once the session is active your page will look like the image below:

![Jupyter Notebook](../_images/ondemand-apps-jupyter-launch.png)

You can choose `Connect to Jupyter` to start your session and create a `New` Python 3 notebook.

![Jupyter Notebook](../_images/ondemand-apps-jupyter-new.png)

This is a new notebook with a familiar interface:

![Jupyter Notebook](../_images/ondemand-apps-jupyter.png)

## Adding virtual environments[¶](#adding-virtual-environments "Permalink to this headline")

To use your own virtual environments in the Jupyter Notebook app, you need to add them externally. Use the following steps to do so:

1. Use the `Remote Desktop` application to log to your CSD3 account, then

Technical Action Required: Yes (staff-managed package availability)

Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
User on SRCP RStudio could not install CRAN packages due to no external internet by design. SRCP policy requires curated package availability managed by staff; users may need to use internal repo mirror and request missing packages.

ACTION
1) Explain SRCP security model (no direct external repo access).
2) Escalate to SRCP team for package provisioning.
3) Ask user for package list.
4) Provide internal repo usage (`options(repos = "http://srcp-r-repo/")`).
5) Track/fork to dedicated package request ticket if needed.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Dear Yira,

On SRCP, direct access to external package repositories is restricted by design. Please use the internal R repo:

options(repos = "http://srcp-r-repo/")
available.packages()

If required packages are still missing, please send the exact package list and we can add them through the SRCP process.

Regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Do not suggest bypassing SRCP network restrictions.

# R and Rscript[¶](#r-and-rscript "Permalink to this headline")

Various versions of the R environment are available on CSD3. The latest
versions are `r/4.1.3/gcc/intel-oneapi-mkl/7dubq4gn` (GTK library support), `r/4.1.3/gcc/intel-oneapi-mkl/wwmppxe3` (vanilla) on the `icelake` partition and `r/4.4.0/gcc/3z5n2tph` on the `cclake` partition.

R can be run interactively by simply calling `R`, which will give a standard
R shell. This can be used for testing or setting up larger runs. However for
use inside the job scheduler it will generally be more effective to record the
R script in a file `analysis.R` and call it from inside a slurm batch script
`Rscript analysis.R`. For more examples of how to use slurm and write an
appropriate batch script please see [Running](../user-guide/quickstart.html#running).

## Installing R packages[¶](#installing-r-packages "Permalink to this headline")

While a few R packages have been centrally installed on CSD3 (do `module avail r-` to see a list), it is often necessary to install extra R packages into your own environment. For example to install cellranger, one can use:

```
$ R
R version...
[...]
> install.packages("cellranger")
```

which will download and build the package from CRAN.

If you run into build errors, the first fix is to tell R where to find the correct compiler. This can be done by creating the file `~/.R/Makevars` in your home directory and adding:

```
CC = gcc
CXX = g++
CXX11 = g++
FC = gfortran
F77 = gfortran
F90 = gfortran
```

which tells R to use the system compilers. If you run into problems with the compiler

# Getting Support on CSD3[¶](#getting-support-on-csd3 "Permalink to this headline")

The University of Cambridge provides support for users on CSD3 and can be contacted via email at support@hpc.cam.ac.uk. This will open a ticket with the support team.

Tip

Each support request should be submitted as a *brand new* email to the address above. If you reopen a previously closed ticket, your request may be missed because it won’t reach the active service desk agent.

To help us help you, please provide as much information as possible in your ticket. At a minimum, please include:

* Your name
* Your account/PI
* your job submission script
* the working directories or paths you are using (e.g. `/rds/user/$USER/hpc-work/foo/bar/`), and
* as much detail as possible on what you are working on, what you have tried, and error messages you receive.

## Use of AI Tools and Online Resources[¶](#use-of-ai-tools-and-online-resources "Permalink to this headline")

We understand that as AI assistants such as ChatGPT, Claude, and Gemini increase in popularity, our users may consult them for assistance with technical issues on our platforms. We have compiled in this section some suggestions on how to safely work with these tools as well as how to engage the service desk.

Before acting on any AI-generated advice, please take a moment to review our guidance below on how to use these tools safely and responsibly.

Note

Much of the advice that follows is equally applicable to non-AI generated content found on the internet.

Please consult the following advice:

* **Understand the limitations:**

shown in the following image:

Fill the job details, then press the button `Launch` at the bottom of the page. This will lead you to your job page.
When the job is starting, the page should look like this:

![RStudio IDE](../_images/ondemand-apps-rstudio-connect.png)

Press the button `Connect to RStudio Server`. This will open the RStudio IDE in a new browser tab, which should look like this:

![RStudio IDE](../_images/ondemand-apps-rstudio-firsttime.png)

The IDE is made of panels and tabs. From the default tab `Console`, 3 panels are visible:

* The actual console, to the left, with a prompt to an interactive R session.
* A first management panel, on the top-right corner, to manage Environments, check your session history or the available tutorials.
* A second management panel, on the bottom-right corner, to manage files, plots, packages and look at plots.

#### Managing user environments and projects on RStudio[¶](#managing-user-environments-and-projects-on-rstudio "Permalink to this headline")

RStudio is a complete IDE that allows direct project creation and management.

To create a new project, select `File` from the top toolbar, then `New project..`, as shown below:

![RStudio IDE](../_images/ondemand-apps-rstudio-newproject.png)

then follows the instructions from the pop-up wizard. It is good practice to create a new folder
for each new project. At the end, a setup file ending with *.Rproj* is created.

Use the console to add new packages to your project, as shown below:

![RStudio

# R and Rscript[¶](#r-and-rscript "Permalink to this headline")

Various versions of the R environment are available on CSD3. The latest
versions are `r/4.1.3/gcc/intel-oneapi-mkl/7dubq4gn` (GTK library support), `r/4.1.3/gcc/intel-oneapi-mkl/wwmppxe3` (vanilla) on the `icelake` partition and `r/4.4.0/gcc/3z5n2tph` on the `cclake` partition.

R can be run interactively by simply calling `R`, which will give a standard
R shell. This can be used for testing or setting up larger runs. However for
use inside the job scheduler it will generally be more effective to record the
R script in a file `analysis.R` and call it from inside a slurm batch script
`Rscript analysis.R`. For more examples of how to use slurm and write an
appropriate batch script please see [Running](../user-guide/quickstart.html#running).

## Installing R packages[¶](#installing-r-packages "Permalink to this headline")

While a few R packages have been centrally installed on CSD3 (do `module avail r-` to see a list), it is often necessary to install extra R packages into your own environment. For example to install cellranger, one can use:

```
$ R
R version...
[...]
> install.packages("cellranger")
```

which will download and build the package from CRAN.

If you run into build errors, the first fix is to tell R where to find the correct compiler. This can be done by creating the file `~/.R/Makevars` in your home directory and adding:

```
CC = gcc
CXX = g++
CXX11 = g++
FC = gfortran
F77 = gfortran
F90 = gfortran
```

which tells R to use the system compilers. If you run into problems with the compiler

# Getting Support on CSD3[¶](#getting-support-on-csd3 "Permalink to this headline")

The University of Cambridge provides support for users on CSD3 and can be contacted via email at support@hpc.cam.ac.uk. This will open a ticket with the support team.

Tip

Each support request should be submitted as a *brand new* email to the address above. If you reopen a previously closed ticket, your request may be missed because it won’t reach the active service desk agent.

To help us help you, please provide as much information as possible in your ticket. At a minimum, please include:

* Your name
* Your account/PI
* your job submission script
* the working directories or paths you are using (e.g. `/rds/user/$USER/hpc-work/foo/bar/`), and
* as much detail as possible on what you are working on, what you have tried, and error messages you receive.

## Use of AI Tools and Online Resources[¶](#use-of-ai-tools-and-online-resources "Permalink to this headline")

We understand that as AI assistants such as ChatGPT, Claude, and Gemini increase in popularity, our users may consult them for assistance with technical issues on our platforms. We have compiled in this section some suggestions on how to safely work with these tools as well as how to engage the service desk.

Before acting on any AI-generated advice, please take a moment to review our guidance below on how to use these tools safely and responsibly.

Note

Much of the advice that follows is equally applicable to non-AI generated content found on the internet.

Please consult the following advice:

* **Understand the limitations:**

metrics.reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Account Entitlements / CPU Access
Technical Action Required: Yes (entitlement check)
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Low

QUICK ASSESSMENT
User on a Tier2 GPU project requested CPU-only runs on icelake. The correct response is to first verify current CPU account entitlements (e.g. via `mybalance`) before advising which account to use.

ACTION
1) Verify user’s current account entitlements (`mybalance` or equivalent account view).
2) If an SL3 CPU account is present, instruct user to submit CPU jobs with that account.
3) If no CPU account is present, provide the correct request/provisioning path.
4) Clarify SL2 path for larger/longer CPU requirements where applicable.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Hi James,

Please check your available project accounts with:
`mybalance`

If you see an SL3 CPU account there, you can use that for CPU-only runs on the relevant CPU partition.

If you need larger/longer CPU resources than SL3 allows (or no CPU account is listed), please request the appropriate SL2 CPU access via your group lead.

Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Confirm actual entitlement list before advising new provisioning.

ACTION
To submit CPU-only jobs on the icelake nodes, the user should use the partition icelake and specify the project t2-cs181-XXX in the Slurm submission script or on the command line [1]. The user should also ensure that they have sufficient compute unit hours available for their project, which can be checked using the mybalance command [1].
